title: Git Sync: Manage Dashboards as Code

blocks.content:
Grafana's Git Sync lets you connect a Git repository to your Grafana instance so dashboards are automatically stored and versioned as code. Changes flow both ways — edit in Grafana and push to Git, or commit to Git and pull into Grafana.

In this guide you'll inspect an active Git Sync connection, create a dashboard that syncs to a repository, and then clean it up.
You'll open the Provisioning page and explore the status of a connected Git repository.
You'll navigate to the provisioning settings and inspect the health, resources, and pull status of an existing Git Sync connection.
Open **Provisioning** from the administration menu.
blocks.tooltip: Provisioning is where you manage external sources of Grafana resources.

blocks.content: Click **View** to open the connected repository.
blocks.tooltip: This repository is already connected and syncing dashboards with Git.

blocks.content: The **Resources** card shows the types and counts of Grafana resources managed by this repository.
blocks.tooltip: Resources include dashboards, folders, and other provisioned items.

blocks.content: The **Health** card reports whether the connection to the remote repository is healthy.
blocks.tooltip: A healthy status means Grafana can reach the repository and authenticate.

blocks.content: The **Pull status** card shows whether the latest pull from the remote repository succeeded and when the last sync job ran.
blocks.tooltip: Grafana periodically pulls changes from the repository on a schedule.

blocks.content: Switch to the **Resources** tab to see every resource managed by this repository.
blocks.tooltip: Each row shows a resource title, type, and its current sync status.

blocks.content: Click **Pull** to manually trigger a sync from the remote repository right now.
blocks.tooltip: This fetches the latest commits and applies any changes to Grafana.

blocks.content: You've seen how Grafana tracks the health, resources, and sync status of a connected Git repository.
Next, you'll create a new dashboard and save it into the Git-synced folder so it automatically appears in the repository.

blocks.title: Add a dashboard to the synced folder

blocks.content: You'll create a dashboard from a template and save it into the Git Sync folder so it's automatically pushed to the connected repository.
Navigate to the synced dashboards folder and create a new dashboard from a template.

steps.description: Open Dashboards from the navigation menu.

steps.reftarget: div[data-testid='data-testid browse-dashboards-table'] div[data-testid='data-testid browse dashboards row git-sync-dashboards/']

blocks.content: Save the dashboard into the **git-sync-dashboards** folder. Because this folder is managed by Git Sync, the dashboard is automatically committed and pushed to the repository.

steps.reftarget: div[data-testid='data-testid dashboard controls'] button[data-testid='data-testid Save dashboard button']
steps.tooltip: Open the save dialog to choose where to store this dashboard.
Open the folder picker to select the target folder.
Save the dashboard into the synced folder.

blocks.content: Open the GitHub repository to verify that your new dashboard has been synced. Once you see the dashboard file on the **main** branch, return to this guide to continue.
blocks.tooltip: Git Sync pushes the dashboard JSON to the repository automatically.

blocks.content: You've created a dashboard and confirmed it was automatically synced to the connected Git repository.
Finally, you'll clean up by deleting the dashboard you just created and verify that the deletion syncs back to the repository.
You'll select and delete the dashboard you just created. The deletion is also synced to the Git repository.
Navigate back to the **git-sync-dashboards** folder.
blocks.tooltip: Use the breadcrumb to return to the folder view.

blocks.content: Select the dashboard you just created by clicking the checkbox.
blocks.tooltip: Selecting a dashboard enables the bulk action toolbar.

blocks.content: Delete the selected dashboard and close the confirmation drawer.
You've removed the dashboard. Git Sync will commit the deletion to the repository, keeping Grafana and Git in sync.

**Congratulations!** You've experienced the full Git Sync workflow — inspecting a connected repository, creating a dashboard that automatically syncs to Git, and cleaning up with a deletion that flows back to the repository. Git Sync makes it easy to manage dashboards as code without leaving the Grafana UI.
